document.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Social Enterprise Collaboration Configuration Guide

root.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Social Enterprise Collaboration Configuration Guide
root.summary: Introduces the Cúram 8.1.2 Social Enterprise Collaboration Configuration Guide. The section explains Merative SPM Social Enterprise Collaboration as a common platform for multidisciplinary agencies, providers, and community partners to improve client and family outcomes. It covers the main tools and concepts: MDT Portal, Social Enterprise Folder (SEF), Citizen Context Viewer (CCV), collaborative outcome plans, collaborative investigation cases, and the deprecated Cúram Case and Participant Index (CPI). It also describes why multidisciplinary teams are needed, how predefined vs. case-specific teams collaborate, and how sharing, meetings, discussions, notes, attachments, and access controls work. CPI is presented as a deprecated cross-system data repository for person and case indexing, search, retrieval, and modernization support for legacy systems. Front matter includes edition information for Cúram 8.1/8.1.1/8.1.2, contents, notices, privacy policy, trademarks, and legal usage terms.

children.title: Note
children.summary: Introductory notice pages. Advises readers to review the “Notices” section before using the information or product, and includes Merative US L.P. copyright information (2012, 2024).

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: Edition information for Merative Cúram 8.1/8.1.1/8.1.2, with copyright and trademark notices. The pages then begin the Cúram Social Enterprise Collaboration Guide: purpose of social enterprise collaboration, key tools (MDT Portal, SEF, CCV), and how agencies, providers, and community members collaborate to improve family outcomes. It introduces multidisciplinary teams, their benefits and types, and collaboration features like discussion boards, meeting scheduling, meeting minutes, and incident reporting, plus process-driven MDT portals.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-24
children.summary: Pages 15–24 explain multidisciplinary team (MDT) collaboration and Social Enterprise Folder (SEF) behavior. They cover predefined vs ad hoc teams, member history, and sharing governance for notes, attachments, and outcome plan activities. The section introduces SEFs as cross-agency case hubs, their agency-worker and non-agency views, client/case histories, and sensitivity-based access controls for cases, clients, meetings, discussions, incidents, notes, and related folders. It also details collaborative outcome plans: automatic creation when sharing is enabled, MDT Portal access, and sharing workflows for notes, attachments, and activities.

children.title: Edition — pages 25-30
children.summary: Covers collaborative investigation case sharing and MDT Portal behavior, including predefined vs ad hoc multidisciplinary teams, configuration of sharing for notes/attachments, notifications, and collaboration tab pages (Multidisciplinary Team, Discussions). Also introduces deprecated CPI: its goal as a central case/person data index, supported data types, search/retrieval across systems, and use in social enterprise folders and modernization from legacy systems.

Edition pages 5–30 for Merative Cúram 8.1/8.1.1/8.1.2. Includes copyright/trademark notices and the Cúram Social Enterprise Collaboration Guide: social enterprise collaboration goals, stakeholders, MDTs, MDT Portal/SEF/CCV, outcome plans, investigation case sharing, and the deprecated CPI. Covers team types and benefits, SEF access controls, sharing workflows for notes/attachments/meetings/discussions, and CPI’s cross-system case/person indexing, search, retrieval, and modernization support.

children.title: Notices
Privacy policy
children.summary: This section covers legal notices: fictitious example data, sample code copyright/license terms, warranty and liability disclaimers, the Merative privacy policy link, and trademark attributions for Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft, and UNIX. Useful for locating licensing, privacy, and branding information.

children.title: Trademarks
children.summary: Lists trademark and copyright notices for Merative and third-party brands (IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft, UNIX), plus a privacy policy link. Also states sample names in the document are fictitious, sample programs are provided “AS IS” under the Merative Client Agreement, and may be copied/modified/distributed for API-conforming development. Includes liability disclaimer and © Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024.

Legal notices and usage terms for the publication: personal and enterprise reproduction is allowed only if proprietary notices are preserved, while redistribution, derivative works, and external use require Merative consent. It includes warranty/liability disclaimers, export compliance, patent notice, third-party product and website disclaimers, and “AS-IS” availability. Also covers fictitious sample data, privacy policy reference, and trademark/copyright attributions, including © Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024.

children.title: Contents
children.summary: Table of contents for the Cúram Social Enterprise Collaboration Guide. Lists front matter plus Chapter 1 sections on social enterprise collaboration, multidisciplinary teams, the Social Enterprise Folder, collaborative outcome plans, collaborative investigation cases, and a deprecated CPI section. Also includes notices, privacy policy, trademarks, and copyright/edition references.

children.title: 1 Cúram Social Enterprise Collaboration Guide
1.1 What Is Social Enterprise Collaboration?
Who Are the People Involved?
children.summary: This section explains who participates in social enterprise collaboration: agencies, government funders, service providers, and community advocates. It gives examples such as employment agencies, training centers, doctors, foster care facilities, addiction clinics, teachers, coaches, school psychologists, employers, godparents, friends, and NGOs. It also introduces how Cúram supports these participants through the MDT Portal, Social Enterprise Folder (SEF), and Citizen Context Viewer (CCV) for communication, case management, and shared family information.

children.title: How Does Cúram Fit this Model?
children.summary: Explains how Cúram supports social enterprise collaboration through COTS tools: the MDT Portal for communication, discussion boards, meetings, and minutes among skilled professionals and community members; the Social Enterprise Folder (SEF) as a central hub for managing clients, cases, outcome plans, and investigations; and the Citizen Context Viewer (CCV) as a tree-structured client summary view. Frames the broader model as involving agencies, funders, service providers, community advocates, and NGOs working together to improve family outcomes.

Defines social enterprise collaboration as a new, holistic approach for agencies and other stakeholders to work together with clients and families to identify root causes and improve outcomes. It emphasizes open communication across agencies, family members, and service providers to reduce duplication, prevent fraud, and match services to needs. The section contrasts this with traditional per-client, per-program delivery and highlights the need for cross-agency visibility and a wide range of benefits and services to support lasting change.

children.title: 1.2 Multidisciplinary teams
Benefits of Working in Teams
children.summary: This section explains why working in teams is valuable for social enterprise collaboration. It says families often have complex needs that require coordinated, innovative solutions, and notes that poor coordination can make it hard to track client progress and may increase long-term dependency on assistance. It introduces the broader set of participants involved, including agencies, governments, service providers, community advocates, and NGOs, and describes how Cúram tools support multidisciplinary collaboration and shared access to client information.

children.title: Types of MDTs And Their Uses
children.summary: This section explains the two MDT types in Cúram social enterprise collaboration: predefined MDTs and individual MDTs. Predefined teams are configured centrally, reused across multiple families/cases, and suited to case types with fixed goals such as domestic abuse investigations. Individual MDTs are created within a folder for one family and can change over time to reflect evolving needs and relationships. It also notes how team composition, updates, and coordination support collaboration in complex cases.

children.title: How MDTs Collaborate Together
Posting Discussions
children.summary: This section explains discussion boards as a collaboration tool for multidisciplinary teams. Members can post comments and questions on a specific subject and respond over time, enabling ongoing online discussion. The purpose is to improve coordination and teamwork among MDT members and agency staff.

children.title: Scheduling Meetings
children.summary: This section explains meeting scheduling in Cúram Social Enterprise Collaboration. Any non-agency multidisciplinary team member or agency worker can schedule meetings with clients, team members, agency workers, and other relevant people. It describes common meeting purposes, such as sharing case information, reviewing case complexity, and discussing issues or concerns. It also covers rescheduling by editing date/time, notifying all invitees even those who previously declined, and canceling meetings with calendar-removal emails.

children.title: Recording Meeting Minutes
children.summary: This section explains the “Record Meeting Minutes” wizard for capturing meeting details and issuing minutes to attendees and other interested parties. It emphasizes that all wizard steps are optional, supports work-in-progress minutes, and lets users record information either all at once or over time. It also introduces related meeting management features such as scheduling, rescheduling, and canceling meetings, with email notifications sent to invitees.

children.title: Reporting Incidents
children.summary: Incidents are defined as events that have, or could have, a direct negative effect on a client’s health and safety. Reporting an incident requires a detailed description, the date and time it occurred, names of affected clients, participant contact details such as witnesses, and any supporting documentation. Files can be attached to provide evidence and other incident-related documentation.

This section summarizes Cúram’s MDT collaboration tools: social enterprise folders as the hub for sharing information, discussion boards for ongoing comments and email notifications, meeting scheduling for clients and team members with rescheduling/canceling support, and the record meeting minutes wizard for capturing and issuing minutes in optional steps. It also notes incident reporting as part of collaboration, where incidents are documented to track risks and prevent serious harm.

children.title: Process-driven MDT Portals
children.summary: Describes process-driven MDT portals for multidisciplinary team collaboration with agency workers and families. The section says these portals help MDT members coordinate via guided processes. It appears after content on meetings and incidents, but this node itself mainly introduces the purpose of MDT portals rather than detailing specific functions.

children.title: Redefining Multidisciplinary Teams
children.summary: Explains how multidisciplinary teams are redefined in Cúram, contrasting predefined teams with individual teams. Predefined teams are kept consistent across folders, can only be changed by an administrator, and may be swapped in a folder with history preserved. Individual teams are more flexible: members can be added from existing MDT members or agency users, and removed except for the lead member until reassigned. A history of prior members is kept for continuity and follow-up. Also notes sharing outcome plan activities, notes, and attachments with non-agency team members.

children.title: Sharing Outcome Plan Activities, Notes & Attachments
children.summary: Explains how outcome plan activities, notes, and attachments can be shared with non-agency multidisciplinary team members through the collaborative outcome plan. It also lists MDT portal access: clients and cases, meetings and minutes, incidents, team members, assigned actions, and outcome plan activities. Useful for understanding collaboration features and what MDT members can view and contribute.

Explains how multidisciplinary teams support social enterprise collaboration in Cúram. It covers why teams are needed for complex family cases, the people involved, and the two MDT types: predefined teams for consistent, reusable case support, and individual teams for one family that can change over time. It also summarizes collaboration tools in MDT portals and social enterprise folders, including discussion boards, meetings, minutes, incident reporting, and sharing outcome plan activities, notes, and attachments with non-agency members.

children.title: 1.3 Social Enterprise Folder
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces the Social Enterprise Folder (SEF) as a case type that centralizes client and case information from multiple agencies and systems. It serves as a secure hub for agency workers and multidisciplinary team members to access, share, and collaborate on information about multiple clients and families. The SEF integrates with the Cúram Case and Participant Index (CPI) to combine data from disparate sources into one view, reducing the need to access many separate systems.

children.title: SEF For Agency Workers
Clients
children.summary: This section explains the SEF “Clients” functionality for agency workers. It covers how clients are added, removed, and tracked in history, and distinguishes fully registered Cúram clients from CPI-based clients sourced from non-Cúram systems. It notes that Cúram-registered clients can be viewed/updated through Participant Manager, while CPI clients expose only CPI-stored data, which can be extended by customers. It also briefly introduces cases in SEFs and their Cúram vs non-Cúram/CPI handling.

children.title: Cases
children.summary: This section explains how cases are handled within a Social Enterprise Framework (SEF). Cases may be either fully Cúram-managed or sourced from non-Cúram systems via the CPI. Agency workers with appropriate security can access Cúram-managed cases directly from the SEF, while non-Cúram cases expose only CPI-stored information. It notes that CPI data can be extended by customers to include case-specific details.

children.title: Multidisciplinary Team
children.summary: Describes how SEFs use multidisciplinary teams: predefined teams managed by administrators versus ad hoc teams created by SEF users with agency and non-agency members. Covers team editing rules, history of team changes, and viewing member contact details, other team assignments, and skills. Also notes non-agency members can access SEF info via the MDT Portal. Includes related SEF features for incidents, calendars, meetings, meeting minutes, invitations, attendance, assigned actions, and issuing minutes to non-agency members.

children.title: Incidents
children.summary: The SEF’s Incidents section lists incidents involving any SEF client in any role (for example, affected client, reporter, or witness). Users can view all incident details from within the SEF. This section is part of the broader SEF context and links to participant incident information in the Cúram Participant Guide.

children.title: Calendars, Meetings & Meeting Minutes
children.summary: This section explains SEF calendar and meeting support. It describes how scheduled SEF meetings can be viewed in calendar or list form, how non-agency multidisciplinary team members can be invited, and how meeting minutes can be recorded. It also notes that attendance can be tracked, actions can be assigned to non-agency team members and shown in the MDT Portal, and meeting minutes can be issued to those members.

children.title: Discussions
children.summary: This section explains SEF discussion boards for multidisciplinary team members and how SEFs can be manually or automatically related when they share clients or issues. It also notes standard case management functions available in a SEF (notes, attachments, communications, status and transaction history, user roles, interactions). Finally, it begins the non-agency worker view via the MDT Portal, describing their restricted access to clients and cases, including limited demographics and no ability to add/remove clients.

children.title: Related SEFs
children.summary: This section explains SEF relationships and related functionality. SEFs can be linked when they share a client or similar issues; users can manually create these links, and new SEFs automatically relate to existing SEFs involving the same clients. It also notes standard case management features available in a SEF (notes, attachments, communications, status and transaction history, user roles, interactions). The section then begins describing what non-agency workers can access through the MDT Portal, including client and case lists with limited demographics and read-only access.

children.title: Standard Case Management Functions
children.summary: This section lists standard case management features available within a SEF, pointing to notes, attachments, communications, status history, transaction history, user roles, and interactions. It also notes that SEFs can be related to other SEFs, either manually or automatically when a new SEF shares clients with an existing one. The section then transitions to SEF access for non-agency workers via the MDT Portal, where they can collaborate securely but have limited permissions.

This section describes the agency worker view of SEFs. It explains how agency users create and manage SEFs, add/remove clients and cases, and track history. It distinguishes Cúram-managed versus CPI-based clients/cases, including access to Participant Manager and CPI-stored data. It also covers multidisciplinary team setup (predefined or ad hoc), team history and member details, incidents, SEF calendars/meetings/minutes, and collaboration with non-agency members via the MDT Portal.

children.title: SEF for Non-Agency Workers
Clients
children.summary: Non-agency multidisciplinary team members can view the SEF’s current and past clients in the MDT Portal, including the dates clients were added or removed by agency workers. They cannot add or remove clients themselves. Only limited client demographics are shown: address, phone number, and date of birth.

children.title: Cases
children.summary: This section explains SEF case access for non-agency multidisciplinary team members via the MDT Portal. They can view current and previous cases associated with the SEF, but not add or remove cases. It also notes that non-agency users can see limited client demographics and that SEFs support discussions, related SEF links, and standard case management functions such as notes, attachments, communications, status history, transaction history, user roles, and interactions.

children.title: Notes
children.summary: This section describes what non-agency multidisciplinary team members can do in a SEF: view case reference, type, clients, and status, but not add/remove cases. It covers standard case notes (view/add/update with proper sensitivity), calendars, meetings, and meeting minutes, as well as attachments and discussions with watcher notifications. It also explains incident access: members can view incidents involving SEF clients only when relevant, and can update incidents they created; otherwise, they have view-only access.

children.title: Calendars, Meetings & Meeting Minutes
children.summary: The SEF calendar shows all scheduled meetings in calendar or list view. Non-agency multidisciplinary team members can set up meetings with agency and other non-agency members, and can record meeting minutes for meetings they organized. This section also sits among related SEF features such as notes, attachments, discussions, and incidents, with access limited by sensitivity and involvement.

children.title: Attachments
children.summary: This section describes SEF attachments for non-agency multidisciplinary team members. They can add standard case attachments, and those attachments may be viewed and updated by other non-agency team members and authorized agency workers with the proper sensitivity level. It sits alongside related SEF features such as notes, meetings, discussions, and incidents, but the focus here is specifically attachment access and sharing rules.

children.title: Discussions
children.summary: Non-agency multidisciplinary team members can participate in SEF discussions with other team members. They are automatically added as watchers to new discussions, receive email notifications on new posts and updates, and can stop watching a discussion to stop notifications. This section also sits among related SEF features like notes, calendars, attachments, and incidents, but the discussion-specific content focuses on posting, participation, watcher behavior, and notifications.

children.title: Incidents
children.summary: Non-agency multidisciplinary team members can view incidents involving any SEF client in roles such as affected client, reporter, or witness, but only for clients they are involved with. They cannot view incident information for other clients. If the incident was created by a non-agency multidisciplinary team member, they can update it; otherwise, they have read-only access.

children.title: Multidisciplinary Team
children.summary: Describes the multidisciplinary team list on a Social Enterprise Folder (SEF), including each member’s role, phone, and email, plus lead-member permissions for adding members and reassigning the lead. Also explains SEF information security via sensitivity levels: users can only access content at or below their sensitivity, with sensitive items asterisked out. Covers sensitivity rules for attachments, meetings, cases, clients, and communications, including constraints on creating or viewing these components.

Explains what non-agency multidisciplinary team members can access in a SEF through the MDT Portal. They can view current/past clients and cases, with only limited client demographics and no ability to add/remove clients or cases. They can add notes and attachments, create meetings and minutes, participate in discussions, and view incidents involving SEF clients when relevant; update rights depend on who created the incident. The section also notes SEF-related calendars, discussions, and sensitivity-based access controls.

children.title: Securing Information in a SEF
children.summary: Explains how information in a Social Enterprise Folder (SEF) is secured using user sensitivity levels. Only users whose sensitivity is equal to or higher than the item’s assigned sensitivity can view content; restricted items are asterisked out. Covers configuration for multi-client components and details sensitivity rules for attachments, meetings, cases, clients, and communications. Also notes that multidisciplinary team lists show role, phone, and email, and lead non-agency members can add members or change the lead.

SEF is a case type that centralizes client and case data from multiple agencies/systems via CPI, with agency and non-agency views. It covers SEF lifecycle, creation by agency workers, adding/removing clients and cases, team setup (predefined or ad hoc), incidents, calendars/meetings/minutes, discussions, related SEFs, and standard case functions. Non-agency members use the MDT Portal with limited access and can add notes, attachments, meetings, minutes, and discussions. It also explains sensitivity-based security and sharing governance for SEF information.

children.title: 1.4 Collaborative Outcome Plan
Configuring an Outcome Plan for Sharing
Sharing Notes & Attachments
children.summary: Explains how outcome plan sharing works for notes, attachments, and activities in Cúram MDT Portal. Agency workers can share notes/attachments and activities with eligible non-agency team members when sharing is enabled; the available list excludes restricted members/roles. For collaborative plans, non-agency members can add items, but the plan owner decides further sharing. Recipients get email links to view items in the MDT Portal after authentication. Activity sharing includes restrictions for sensitive services/actions and displays key activity details, with referral-specific provider information.

children.title: Sharing Activities
children.summary: Explains how outcome plan activities (services, actions, referrals) can be shared with non-agency multidisciplinary team members when sharing is enabled. Covers the extra sharing step in activity wizards, eligibility filtering by member/role and admin-defined restrictions for sensitive activities, and email notifications with MDT Portal access. Also notes what activity details are visible when shared, varying by activity type.

Explains how outcome plans are configured for sharing in Cúram: sharing can be enabled or disabled per plan type, which adds a Collaboration tab and creates a collaborative outcome plan for non-agency MDT members. Covers predefined vs ad hoc multidisciplinary teams, access via MDT Portal, and histories of team changes. Also describes sharing notes/attachments and activities: agency workers choose eligible members, role-based restrictions can exclude recipients, emails notify recipients, and shared activity details vary by type, including referral provider info.

children.title: Collaborative Outcome Plan Features
children.summary: Describes Collaborative Outcome Plan features available to non-agency MDT Portal users: shared activities, notes, meeting minutes, meetings, discussions, attachments, and team details. Non-agency members can view shared services/actions/referrals, add notes to services and actions, and see notes, minutes, documents, and schedules related to the outcome plan. The section then begins introducing Collaborative Investigation Case, an automatically created, non-manual SEF for non-agency collaborators to view a shared investigation subset.

Explains Collaborative Outcome Plans in Cúram: a system-created, non-manual SEF for non-agency MDT members to view a shared subset of an outcome plan via the MDT Portal. Covers when sharing is enabled, Collaboration tab/pages, predefined vs ad hoc multidisciplinary teams, team history, and sharing controls for notes, attachments, activities, and discussions. Describes how agency workers select eligible recipients, role-based restrictions, email notifications, and what activity/referral details are shared.

children.title: 1.5 Collaborative Investigation Case
Configuring an Investigation Case for Sharing
Sharing Notes & Attachments
children.summary: Explains how notes and attachments are shared in collaborative investigation cases when sharing is enabled. Agency workers can select which non-agency multidisciplinary team members may view a new note or attachment, with some roles excluded if sharing is restricted. Non-agency members receive email notification and access the item through the MDT Portal. Non-agency members can also add notes/attachments, but the investigation case owner decides who else can see them. Lists collaborative case features: notes, meeting minutes, meetings, discussions, attachments, and team details.

Explains how to configure an investigation type for sharing in Cúram Administration. If sharing is enabled, creating an investigation case also creates a collaborative investigation case for non-agency multidisciplinary team members, who access it through the MDT Portal. Covers team setup options: predefined teams (administrator-managed, with history) and ad hoc teams (user-created, editable, with history). Also notes that the Collaboration tab appears with Multidisciplinary Team and Discussions pages, and that sharing primarily controls visibility of notes and attachments.

children.title: Collaborative Investigation Case Features
children.summary: Describes collaborative investigation case sharing behavior and portal features. If sharing is disabled, the Collaboration tab and related pages are hidden. When enabled, agency workers can share new notes and attachments with selected non-agency MDT members, excluding roles not permitted. Non-agency members can add notes/attachments, but the case owner decides further sharing. The section lists case features visible in the collaborative case: Notes, Meeting Minutes, Meetings, Discussions, Attachments, and Team details.

Explains collaborative investigation cases in Cúram: a system-created MDT Portal view of an investigation used by non-agency team members, never created manually. If sharing is enabled for an investigation type, the Collaboration tab appears with Multidisciplinary Team and Discussions pages; if disabled, it is hidden. Covers configuring predefined vs ad hoc teams, shared notes/attachments controls, role-based exclusions, and notification/sharing workflow. Visible features include Notes, Meeting Minutes, Meetings, Discussions, Attachments, and Team details.

children.title: 1.6 (deprecated) Cúram Case and Participant Index (CPI)
(deprecated) The Goals of CPI
children.summary: Deprecated overview of the Cúram Case and Participant Index (CPI). Explains its goal: break down siloed social service systems by providing a central repository for case/client data, enabling a holistic view across systems, families, and legacy sources. Describes how CPI supports Social Enterprise Folders and the Citizen Context Viewer, and lists its three core functions: store data from multiple systems, enable searches across Cúram and non-Cúram systems, and retrieve/display data in SEF/CCV. Includes examples of stored data types such as person details and relationships.

children.title: (deprecated) How CPI Works
(deprecated) Store Data from Systems in CPI
children.summary: This deprecated section explains CPI’s purpose as a central data repository for breaking up siloed case/client systems and giving agencies a holistic view of individuals and families. It describes CPI’s main functions: storing data from multiple systems, enabling search across stored data, and supporting retrieval for SEF and CCV. It also introduces how systems must be configured to send data to CPI and lists out-of-the-box data types such as person details and person relationships.

children.title: (deprecated) Search for Data Stored in CPI
children.summary: Describes deprecated CPI search functionality. External systems linked to the CPI data repository can search cases and persons using subsets of criteria, with CPI IDs assigned to stored records and matching rules to reduce duplicates. Examples include person searches across systems using name, SSN, and address, then filtering by source system. Also notes CPI retrieval/display in social enterprise folders and citizen context viewers, plus using CPI configuration to support gradual modernization from legacy systems to Cúram by enabling or disabling selected data types.

children.title: (deprecated) Retrieve CPI Data and Display
children.summary: Explains how Social Enterprise Collaboration uses CPI data for shared case and person views across agencies. It covers the kinds of CPI data exposed in folders and citizen context viewers, how external system records can be added as clients/cases, and how relationships between persons from different systems can be recorded. Also notes CPI’s role in modernization: configurable on/off support for migrating person and case data from legacy systems to Cúram, with a pointer to the developer guide for technical details.

Deprecated CPI overview: explains that Cúram Case and Participant Index is a central repository meant to break up siloed case/client systems, give agencies a holistic view across individuals and families, and help modernize legacy data into Cúram. It defines three core functions: store data from multiple systems, let Cúram and non-Cúram systems search CPI data, and retrieve/display data in Social Enterprise Folder and Citizen Context Viewer. Also notes systems must be configured to send data to CPI and lists supported out-of-box data types like person details and person relationships.

children.title: (deprecated) Using CPI Configuration to Support Modernization
children.summary: Deprecated guidance on using CPI configuration to support modernization in Cúram/SEC. Explains that CPI can help migrate from legacy systems to Cúram gradually by enabling or disabling specific CPI data types, so agencies can keep some data constant while migrating others. Example given: display person information but not case information, or migrate program by program. This section is mainly an overview and points technical readers to the Case Participant Index Developer Guide.

Deprecated overview of the Cúram Case and Participant Index (CPI). Explains CPI as a central repository for case/client data that breaks down siloed social service systems and supports a holistic view of individuals and families. Covers the three core functions: store data from multiple systems, search CPI data from Cúram and non-Cúram systems, and retrieve/display data in Social Enterprise Folder and Citizen Context Viewer. Lists example CPI data types and describes how CPI configuration can support gradual modernization from legacy systems.

Introduces Cúram Social Enterprise Collaboration as a platform for multidisciplinary agencies, providers, and community partners to work together for better family outcomes. Summarizes the main tools: MDT Portal, Social Enterprise Folder, Citizen Context Viewer, collaborative outcome plans, collaborative investigations, and CPI. Explains the collaboration model, why teams are needed, and the difference between predefined and individual MDTs, including sharing, meetings, discussions, notes, and security. Also includes a deprecated overview of CPI’s repository, search, retrieval, and modernization role.
